children.name: Podargus
children.description: Large, robust frogmouths with broad, heavy bills and a cryptic grey-brown plumage that mimics broken branches. They are nocturnal, perch-hunting predators endemic to Australasia.

children.name: Podargus strigoides
children.description: Australia's most familiar frogmouth, easily recognised by its wide froglike mouth and excellent camouflage that makes it look like a broken tree stump. It is a nocturnal predator that catches insects, small mammals and frogs by sallying from a perch. Pairs form lifelong bonds and maintain territories across most of Australia.

children.name: Podargus papuensis
children.description: The largest frogmouth in the world, reaching up to 60 centimetres in length. It is found across New Guinea and the Cape York Peninsula of northern Australia, inhabiting rainforest edges and mangroves. Its deep, booming call is often described as a repeated 'oom-oom-oom' that resonates through the night.

children.name: Podargus ocellatus
children.description: A smaller, intricately patterned frogmouth with fine vermiculations and distinct white spots across its wings. It occurs in the rainforests of New Guinea and the Solomon Islands, with a small and vulnerable population in Queensland's Wet Tropics. Its plumage so perfectly mimics lichen-covered bark that it is nearly invisible during the day.

children.name: Batrachostomus
children.description: Smaller, more delicately built frogmouths with ornate, finely patterned plumage that often includes white spots, bars and frilled feather edges. They are found in tropical forests across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Batrachostomus hodgsoni
children.description: A small frogmouth of the eastern Himalayan foothills, northern Myanmar and southern China, where it inhabits subtropical broadleaf forests. Its plumage is a rich chestnut-brown with bold white spots on the wings and a finely barred breast. It is rarely seen, its presence usually betrayed only by its soft, whistling call at night.

children.name: Batrachostomus moniliger
children.description: Endemic to the Western Ghats of India and Sri Lanka, this tiny frogmouth measures just 23 centimetres and has a distinctive 'necklace' of white spots across the upper breast. Males are greyish-brown while females are richer rufous. It is a bird of the dense, humid understorey, where it sits motionless on exposed perches after dark.
